---
title: "04 · Addition and Subtraction"
subtitle: "Two operations, one picture: walking along the number line"
lesson-id: "t1.add-subtract"
tier: 1
prerequisites: ["t1.number-line", "t1.place-value"]
unlocks: ["t1.multiply-as-area", "t1.negative-numbers", "t1.intro-variables"]
applied-domains: ["systems", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Are you ready for this lesson?

1. Can you locate any number — say **7** — on the number line, from
   [Lesson 2](../02-number-line/index.qmd)?
2. Can you read **243** as "two hundred and forty-three" and say what each
   digit is worth, from [Lesson 3](../03-place-value/index.qmd)?
3. Have you done addition and subtraction by hand at some point — even if
   you found it mechanical?

If yes to (1) and (2) you are ready. The point of this lesson is not to
*teach* you to add and subtract — it is to *re-ground* what you already do.
:::

## Touch

Use the slider to set a starting position. Then press the buttons to walk
right (add) or left (subtract). Watch the number-line marker hop, and watch
the running total update.

```{ojs}
viewof startPos = Inputs.range([0, 20], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "Start at:"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof step = Inputs.range([1, 9], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "Step size:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 720, H = 130, pad = 30;
  const xMin = -5, xMax = 30;
  const x = v => pad + ((v - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * (W - 2 * pad);
  const ticks = d3.range(xMin, xMax + 1, 5);

  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");

  // Axis
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad)
    .attr("y1", H/2).attr("y2", H/2)
    .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);

  // Ticks
  for (const t of ticks) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", x(t)).attr("x2", x(t))
      .attr("y1", H/2 - 6).attr("y2", H/2 + 6)
      .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 1.4);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", x(t)).attr("y", H/2 + 24)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13").text(t);
  }

  // Sum after stepping
  const sumPos = startPos + step;
  const diffPos = startPos - step;

  // Arc showing add (right)
  const arcRight = (from, to, color, label) => {
    const xf = x(from), xt = x(to);
    const mid = (xf + xt) / 2, height = 30;
    svg.append("path")
      .attr("d", `M ${xf},${H/2 - 4} Q ${mid},${H/2 - height} ${xt},${H/2 - 4}`)
      .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", color).attr("stroke-width", 2);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", mid).attr("y", H/2 - height - 4)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "12").attr("fill", color)
      .text(label);
  };
  arcRight(startPos, sumPos, "#2e7d32", `+${step} → ${sumPos}`);

  // Arc showing subtract (left)
  const arcLeft = (from, to, color, label) => {
    const xf = x(from), xt = x(to);
    const mid = (xf + xt) / 2, height = 30;
    svg.append("path")
      .attr("d", `M ${xf},${H/2 + 4} Q ${mid},${H/2 + height} ${xt},${H/2 + 4}`)
      .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", color).attr("stroke-width", 2);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", mid).attr("y", H/2 + height + 12)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "12").attr("fill", color)
      .text(label);
  };
  arcLeft(startPos, diffPos, "#c2185b", `−${step} → ${diffPos}`);

  // Marker at start
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", x(startPos)).attr("cy", H/2).attr("r", 7)
    .attr("fill", "#2c5f7c").attr("stroke", "#fff").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x(startPos)).attr("y", H/2 - 12)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "12").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .attr("fill", "#2c5f7c").text(startPos);

  return svg.node();
}
```

```{ojs}
{
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; font-family:monospace; font-size:1.1rem; margin-top: 0.5rem;">
    <span style="color:#2e7d32;">${startPos} + ${step} = ${startPos + step}</span>
    &nbsp; · &nbsp;
    <span style="color:#c2185b;">${startPos} − ${step} = ${startPos - step}</span>
  </div>`;
}
```

Adding hops you to the right. Subtracting hops you to the left. *That is
all*. Everything else — carrying, borrowing, columns — is bookkeeping for
when the hops are large.

## Wonder

1. If you start at 12 and hop right by 5, where do you land? If you then
   hop left by 5, where are you?
2. Is "12 + 5" the same as "5 + 12"? What about "12 − 5" and "5 − 12"?
3. When you do addition by hand on paper, *why* do you "carry the 1"? Have
   you ever asked?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Compute these in your head, by walking the number line:
$$8 + 6, \quad 13 + 4, \quad 15 - 7$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Picture the marker. Hop right for plus, left for minus.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$8 + 6 = 14$, $\ 13 + 4 = 17$, $\ 15 - 7 = 8$. Each was just a walk along
the line — a sequence of hops. The arithmetic and the picture are the
same thing.
</details>

### Problem 2

Try both orders:
$$12 + 5 \ \text{vs.} \ 5 + 12; \qquad 12 - 5 \ \text{vs.} \ 5 - 12.$$

What do you notice?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For addition: hops are hops; the order doesn't matter. For subtraction: it
might.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$12 + 5 = 17$ and $5 + 12 = 17$** — the same. For addition, the order
doesn't matter (this is called the **commutative** property — but you
discovered it without the word).

**$12 - 5 = 7$ but $5 - 12 = -7$.** They are *not* the same. Subtraction
is sensitive to which number you start from. Hopping left from 12 lands
you at 7. Hopping left from 5 the same distance overshoots 0 — you land
at $-7$ (the negative numbers we met informally in
[Lesson 2](../02-number-line/index.qmd)).
</details>

### Problem 3

Now a *big* one — without a calculator:
$$47 + 35$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Hopping by 1 thirty-five times is silly. Use **place value** —
[Lesson 3](../03-place-value/index.qmd) — to hop in tens and ones.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Add tens to tens (4 + 3 = 7 → 70), add ones to ones (7 + 5 = 12). Then
combine — but watch what happens: 12 ones is *more than 9*, so the ones
column overflows.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Tens: $40 + 30 = 70$. Ones: $7 + 5 = 12$. Combine: $70 + 12 = 82$.

When you write the algorithm vertically:
$$\begin{array}{r} \!\!^{1}\,4 \, 7 \\ +\ 3 \, 5 \\ \hline 8 \, 2 \end{array}$$
the "carry the 1" is exactly this: 12 ones don't fit in the ones column
(only digits 0–9 are allowed there), so the *one ten* of overflow gets
written above the tens column. It's pure place-value bookkeeping.
</details>

### Problem 4

Now subtract: $52 - 17$. What goes wrong if you try column-by-column
naively?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Ones first: $2 - 7$. The ones column doesn't have enough.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2 - 7$ would go negative — but the ones column can only hold digits 0–9.
So we **borrow**: take one ten from the tens column (turning it into ten
ones) and add those to the ones column.

After borrowing: ones column is $12$, tens column has $4$ (was $5$).
Now $12 - 7 = 5$ in ones, $4 - 1 = 3$ in tens. Result: **35**.

Both "carrying" and "borrowing" are the same idea: when a column overflows
or underflows, you re-arrange the place-value bookkeeping. The arithmetic
itself is just walking along the number line.
</details>

### Problem 5

Compute $999 + 1$. Then compute $1000 - 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each one is a single hop along the line. But notice what happens to the
*digits*.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$999 + 1 = 1000$. The ones column overflows (9+1=10), carries 1 to the
tens, which also overflows (9+1=10), carries to hundreds, also overflows,
carries to thousands. **Three carries in a single addition.**

$1000 - 1 = 999$. Mirror image: borrow from the thousands → hundreds → tens
→ ones, three borrows in cascade.

This is what makes large-number arithmetic feel "tricky" — but it is the
*same* tiny rule (overflow goes left, underflow borrows from the left)
applied repeatedly.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Addition** is "hop right". $a + b$ means: start at $a$, take $b$ hops to
the right.

**Subtraction** is "hop left". $a - b$ means: start at $a$, take $b$ hops
to the left.

When numbers are large, we hop in *place-value chunks* (ones, tens,
hundreds) rather than one at a time. **Carrying** records overflow from
one column into the next column on the left. **Borrowing** records that
we took some value from the column on the left to cover an underflow.

Addition is commutative: $a + b = b + a$.
Subtraction is **not**: $a - b \neq b - a$ in general.

:::

> *To add is to walk right; to subtract, to walk left.*
> *Hop in tens and hundreds when the journey is long.*
> *When a column overflows, carry the overflow leftward;*
> *When a column runs short, borrow from the left.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $284 + 567$.

A competent solver works column by column, right to left:

1. **Ones:** $4 + 7 = 11$. Write **1**, carry **1**.
2. **Tens:** $8 + 6 + 1 \,(\text{carried}) = 15$. Write **5**, carry **1**.
3. **Hundreds:** $2 + 5 + 1 \,(\text{carried}) = 8$. Write **8**.

$$\begin{array}{r} \!^{1}\!^{1}\,2 \, 8 \, 4 \\ +\ 5 \, 6 \, 7 \\ \hline 8 \, 5 \, 1 \end{array}$$

**Answer: 851.** Check on the line: starting at 284, walking 567 to the
right, you land at 851. Same operation, different picture.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute by hopping (in your head): $14 + 6$, $\ 25 - 8$, $\ 9 + 11$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$14 + 6 = 20$, $\ 25 - 8 = 17$, $\ 9 + 11 = 20$.
</details>

**W2.** Without computing fully, which is bigger: $200 + 350$ or $300 +
240$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Estimate by hundreds first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$200 + 350 = 550$ and $300 + 240 = 540$. The first is bigger by 10.
Estimating by hundreds (200+300=500 vs 300+200=500) was a tie; the tens
broke it.
</details>

**W3.** Compute $73 - 28$ by hand, showing the borrow.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Ones: $3 - 8$ underflows. Borrow 1 ten from the tens column, leaving 6
tens; ones become 13. $13 - 8 = 5$. Tens: $6 - 2 = 4$. **Answer: 45.**
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A bus has 47 passengers. At the next stop, 19 get off and 12 get
on. How many passengers are on the bus now?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two hops: left 19, then right 12. Or compute the *net* hop first.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$47 - 19 + 12$. Method 1: $47 - 19 = 28$, then $28 + 12 = 40$. Method 2:
net change is $-19 + 12 = -7$, so $47 - 7 = 40$. Either way, **40
passengers**. Notice that the order of operations didn't matter for the
*net* result.
</details>

**P2.** Find the missing number: $\ ? + 36 = 100$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"What plus 36 gives 100?" is the same question as "100 minus 36 is what?"
Subtraction *undoes* addition.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$100 - 36 = 64$. Check: $64 + 36 = 100$. ✓

The deeper idea: addition and subtraction are **inverse** operations —
each undoes the other. We will use this constantly when we meet
[Variables](../11-intro-variables/index.qmd) and start solving equations.
</details>

**P3.** A child has Rs. 1000. She spends Rs. 247 on a book and Rs. 158 on
a notebook. How much is left?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two subtractions, in either order — or one big addition followed by one
subtraction.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Total spent: $247 + 158 = 405$. Money left: $1000 - 405 = 595$.
**Rs. 595 remain.**

Check: $1000 - 247 = 753$, then $753 - 158 = 595$. Same answer; addition
*before* subtraction is more efficient because we collapse two hops into
one.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Without computing the right side, decide whether this is true or
false:
$$48 + 73 = 73 + 48$$

Then decide for:
$$48 - 73 = 73 - 48$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**True for the first; false for the second.** Addition's commutative
property says order does not matter ($a + b = b + a$ for all numbers $a,
b$). Subtraction is not commutative — in fact $a - b$ and $b - a$ have
opposite signs. (We will return to this when we name the **negation**
operation in [Lesson 10: Negative Numbers](../10-negative-numbers/index.qmd).)
</details>

**S2.** A computer adds two numbers stored in 8 binary "columns" (an
8-bit number — recall binary from [Lesson 3](../03-place-value/index.qmd)).
The columns are worth, right to left: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128. The
largest 8-bit number is $11111111$ (all 1s).

What number is that, in our usual base-10? What happens if you try to add
1 to it?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sum the column values: $1 + 2 + 4 + ... + 128$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Sum: $128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 255$. So the largest 8-bit
number is **255**.

Adding 1 to $11111111$ would set the ones column to 10 — overflow! The
carry propagates all the way left, just like our $999 + 1$ in *Discover
P5*. But there is no 9th column to receive the final carry. The result is
either: a 9-bit number $100000000$ (= 256) if you have room, or — in fixed
8-bit storage — the number wraps around to $00000000$ (= 0). This is
called **integer overflow**, and it is the source of many famous bugs in
real software (the Y2K-style flaws, the 2038 problem, and a particularly
funny one in *Civilization* where Gandhi's aggression rolled over to
maximum). Place-value arithmetic is the same; the storage limit is what
bites.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Subtraction always gives a smaller number."** Only when you start
   with a positive number and subtract a positive number. As soon as
   negative numbers enter — Lesson 10 — subtracting a negative *grows*
   the result.
2. **"Carrying / borrowing are different ideas."** They are mirror images
   of the same idea: a column has overflowed (or underflowed), so the
   excess (or deficit) gets recorded in the column to the left.
3. **"Addition and subtraction must be done in the order written."** For
   *only-addition* expressions, you can rearrange freely
   ($3 + 5 + 7 = 7 + 3 + 5$). For mixed addition and subtraction, you
   can rearrange if you carry the *sign* with each number — but that's
   most easily handled after we name negative numbers in
   [Lesson 10](../10-negative-numbers/index.qmd).
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain why "carrying the 1" works, using the words
   "column" and "overflow"?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute $342 + 189$ by hand?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

- **3 yes:** ready for **[Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](../05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** — that one proves you connect place value to
  arithmetic. Then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover, Problems 3 and 4* — those carry the
  weight.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Lesson 2 — The Number Line](../02-number-line/index.qmd)** — addition
  is "step right", subtraction is "step left". The whole lesson lives on
  this picture.
- **[Lesson 3 — Place Value](../03-place-value/index.qmd)** — carrying
  and borrowing *are* place-value bookkeeping.

### What this unlocks

- **[Multiplication as Area](../05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)** —
  multiplication is repeated addition done in two dimensions.
- **[Negative Numbers](../10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)** — subtracting
  more than you have forces the line to extend left of zero.
- **[Variables](../11-intro-variables/index.qmd)** — equations like
  $x + 36 = 100$ are solved by inverting the addition.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Systems.** Every CPU has an "ALU" (arithmetic logic unit) whose primary
job is binary addition with carry. Subtraction is implemented as addition
of a *negation* — the same trick you saw informally in *Discover P2*.
Integer overflow (Stretch S2) is one of the most common sources of
serious software bugs.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** The pair (addition, subtraction) is the first
example of a **group** — an algebraic structure with an operation, an
identity (here, 0), and inverses (here, "negation"). The whole subject of
**abstract algebra** in Tier 6 grows from generalising what you just did.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Banking, accounting, inventory, scoring in games,
measuring time differences, calculating change at a shop — addition and
subtraction are the workhorse operations of daily numerical life. The
algorithms you internalise here run quietly in your head millions of
times across a lifetime.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Pestalozzi (the hop widget makes addition and
  subtraction physical hops), Boole (the Wonder questions ask "why do you
  carry?" — a question almost no one is taught to ask), Russian school
  (the Discover sequence forces the carry/borrow algorithm to fall out of
  place value rather than be presented as ritual), Bhāskara (single-box
  Name + verse), Better Explained (the "Aha!" framing is *carrying is just
  place-value overflow*).
- **Cross-references** to negative numbers and variables are forward
  references but explicitly named as future lessons.
- **Self-study guarantees** all present.
:::
